title: Calibration of stewart platforms and other parallel manipulators by minimizing inverse kinematic residuals
abstract:
Calibration of Stewart
Platforms and Other
Parallel Manipulators
by Minimizing Inverse
Kinematic Residuals

Hanqi Zhuang and Jiahua Yan
Robotics Center and
Department of Electrical Engineering
Florida Atlantic University
Boca Raton, Florida 33431
Oren Masory
Robotics Center and
Department of Mechanical Engineering
Florida Atlantic University
Boca Raton, Florida 33431
Received December 6, 1996; accepted March 3, 1998
This paper focuses on the accuracy enhancement of Stewart platforms through
kinematic calibration. The calibration problem is formulated in terms of a measure-
ment residual, which is the discrepancy between the measured leg length and the
computed leg length. With this formulation, one is able to identify kinematic error
parameters of the Stewart platform without the necessity of solving the forward
kinematic problem; thus avoiding the numerical problems associated with any for-
ward kinematic solution. By this formulation, a concise differential error model with a
well-structured identification Jacobian, which relates the pose measurement residual
to the errors in the parameters of the platform, is derived. Experimental studies
confirmed the effectiveness of the method. It is also shown in this paper that the
proposed approach can be applied to other types of parallel manipulators, assuming
that their inverse kinematic solution is simpler than its forward kinematic solution.
Because this condition is satisfied by almost all parallel manipulators, the method is
very useful for kinematic calibration of such machines.
Q 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.

This paper focuses on the accuracy enhancement of Stewart platforms through
kinematic calibration. The calibration problem is formulated in terms of a measure-
ment residual, which is the discrepancy between the measured leg length and the
computed leg length. With this formulation, one is able to identify kinematic error
parameters of the Stewart platform without the necessity of solving the forward
kinematic problem; thus avoiding the numerical problems associated with any for-
ward kinematic solution. By this formulation, a concise differential error model with a
well-structured identification Jacobian, which relates the pose measurement residual
to the errors in the parameters of the platform, is derived. Experimental studies
confirmed the effectiveness of the method. It is also shown in this paper that the
proposed approach can be applied to other types of parallel manipulators, assuming
that their inverse kinematic solution is simpler than its forward kinematic solution.
Because this condition is satisfied by almost all parallel manipulators, the method is
very useful for kinematic calibration of such machines.

Journal of Robotic Systems—1998

1. INTRODUCTION
Parallel manipulators have attracted considerable
interest in the robotics research community. Two
special issues were published in the
Journal of
Robotics Systems.1, 2 This paper concentrates on the
calibration of parallel manipulators in general and
Stewart platforms in particular.
A Stewart platform is a parallel manipulator
that consists of six variable-length legs connected at
one end to a fixed base by U-joints and at the other
end to a movable plate by ball joints. This structure
offers high forcertorque capability and high struc-
tural rigidity in exchange for a small workspace and
reduced dexterity. As a result, similar platforms
have been used as flight simulators, manipulators,
and recently as computer numerically controlled
Ž
.
3, 4
CNC milling machines.
A typical control strategy for such platforms is
to specify the pose of the plate in world coordinates,
and then to use the inverse kinematics relationship
to solve the individual leg lengths. The accuracy of
the platform pose critically depends on the knowl-
edge of the kinematic model residing in the device
controller. Kinematic calibration is a process by
which the accuracy of the platform can be improved
by orders of magnitude through modifying its kine-
matic model. The issue of kinematic calibration, like
those of kinematic, dynamic control and design, is
important for further applications of Stewart plat-
forms.
Everett and Lin5 investigated kinematic calibra-
tion of manipulators with closed kinematic chains.
This approach is based on constrained optimization
involving a large number of redundant parameters.
In ref. 6, a simple calibration strategy is proposed
exclusively for the calibration of Stewart platforms.
To identify each leg’s kinematic parameters, the
length of the respective leg is kept fixed while the
other legs stretch or shrink. Although this method is
simple, the measurable platform workspace is dras-
tically reduced. Another method that utilizes only
internal joint measurements to achieve autonomous
calibration was proposed in ref. 7. A kinematic
modeling approach, which takes into account man-
ufacturing errors of the Stewart platform, was pre-
sented in ref. 8. Using this model, the effects of
manufacturing tolerances on the platform accuracy
were investigated. In the same investigation, an
error model-based technique, which requires the
undesirable forward kinematic solution, was ap-
plied to the calibration of a Stewart platform and
demonstrated by simulation. Geng and Haynes9
proposed a two-stage calibration procedure in which
the calibration of plate and base joint locations is
decoupled from that of joint offsets. Their procedure
requires fixing a number of leg lengths during pose
measurements, which again drastically reduces the
measurable workspace of the platform.
Stewart platform calibration, similar to the cali-
bration of serial spatial manipulators, normally en-
compasses four tasks: kinematic modeling of the
platform to account for major error sources; mea-
surement of the platform poses; identification of the
kinematic error parameters of the platform by use of
the measurement data; and accuracy compensation
of the platform by use of the identified error param-
eters.
It has been shown in the case of serial manipu-
lators that the error-model based technique is an
effective means of kinematic calibration.10 ]12 This
approach will be adopted for the development of an
error-model based technique for the calibration of
Stewart platforms. This paper focuses on the accu-
racy enhancement of Stewart platforms through
kinematic calibration. The calibration problem is
formulated in terms of a measurement residual that
is the discrepancy between the measured leg length
and the computed leg length. With this formulation,
one is able to identify kinematic error parameters of
the Stewart platform without the necessity of solv-
ing the forward kinematic problem, thus avoiding
the numerical problems associated with any for-
ward kinematic solution. The error parameters are
basically the installation errors of the platform ball
and U-joints, as well as the leg length offsets. By
this formulation, a concise differential error model
is derived, with a well-structured identification Ja-
cobian relating the pose measurement residual to
the errors in the parameters of the platform. It is
also shown in this paper that the proposed ap-
proach can be applied to other types of parallel
manipulators, assuming that their inverse kinematic
solution is simpler than their forward kinematic
solution.
This paper is organized as follows. The Stewart
platform calibration problem is stated in Section 2,
followed by the outline of the solution strategy and
the derivation of a kinematic error model in Section
3. Section 4 extends the proposed approach to other
parallel
manipulators.
Experimental
results
ob-
tained with the FAU Stewart platform are presented
in Section 5. Section 6 provides conclusions and
some final thoughts.

The parallel robot, illustrated in Figure 1, is com-
posed of six variable-length legs, a base, and a
movable plate. Each leg is connected to the base by
a U-joint and to the plate by a ball joint. A base
B is placed at an arbitrary loca-

perpendicular to the nominal base plane. Similarly,

the base frame to the world frame are fixed and
application dependent, these are omitted for better
clarity and without loss of generality.

The actual leg length is the sum of the nominal joint

It must be stressed that the above assumption is

The kinematic identification problem is to be formu-
lated as a nonlinear least-squares problem. Once
this is done, a standard gradient-based algorithm
can be applied to seek its optimal solution.
Journal of Robotic Systems—1998
One possible way to construct a cost function for the
prescribed problem is to use the norm of the pose
errors of the platform. However, this formulation
will require the forward kinematic solution of the
platform, which is considered to be complex. An-
other, less important problem is that scaling is nec-
essary to balance the contribution of position errors
and orientation errors in the cost function. To avoid
these problems, one may formulate the cost func-
tion based solely on the inverse kinematic solution.
sured joint displacements of leg i at the platform
pose j, respectively. Here the computed joint dis-
placements are obtained by applying the inverse
kinematic solution of the platform, using the nomi-
nal values of the kinematic parameters and the
measurement residual , which is the difference be-
tween the measured and the computed lengths of
leg i at pose j. Accordingly, the following cost
function is defined in terms of the measurement
residuals of all legs at all measured poses:
Thus the Stewart platform calibration problem
is then reduced to minimizing C by selecting the
kinematic parameters of the platform.
The nonlinear optimization problems described by
4 may be solved by standard gradient-based non-

available. In this section, an error model that facili-

Based on the above derivation, the following Stew-

One can now solve for the 42 error parameters

The process is iterated until certain terminal condi-
Note that the identification Jacobian is a block
diagonal matrix; therefore, error parameters of each
leg can be solved independently. In this way, one
can improve the numerical efficiency of the kine-
matic identification algorithm.
Zhuang, Yan, and Masory: Calibration of Stewart Platforms
Another issue is the observability or identifia-
of the error parameters. It is said that the
error parameters are observable if the rank of the
identification Jacobian is full.15 It is difficult in this
case to derive symbolically the conditions for the
observability of the error parameters. However, one
can compute the rank of the identification Jacobian
numerically in an experimental study. Moreover,
this identification Jacobian is handy if optimal mea-
surement configurations are sought for the im-
provement of the efficiency of kinematic parameter
identification. This is because almost all existing
utilized singular values of an identification Jacobian
to construct cost functions.16 ]18
4. EXTENSION OF THE METHOD TO OTHER
The solution method presented in Sections 2 and 3
can be extended to other types of parallel manipula-

Traditionally, the pose error of the manipulator
is defined in terms of the forward kinematic model
of the manipulator. To minimize pose errors, one
has to solve the forward kinematic problem, which
is complex for many parallel manipulators.
Assume that the inverse kinematic problem of
the manipulator is simpler than its forward kine-
matic problem. It is advantageous to define the cost
function in terms of joint errors rather than pose
errors. Let the measured joint variable vector of the
manipulator be denoted by q. The cost function can

Clearly, the inverse kinematic model of a ma-
nipulator and its error model are machine-depen-
dent. We have shown here that by adopting the
strategy detailed in Section 3, the computation effort
for kinematic identification of any parallel manipu-
lator can be greatly reduced as long as its inverse
kinematic solution is simpler than its forward kine-
Journal of Robotic Systems—1998
The experimental system includes a Stewart plat-
form that was designed and fabricated at the FAU
theodolite mode Kern-E1 that has a resolution of 2
The Stewart platform is shown in Figure 2. Hy-
draulic pistons that form the links of the platform
are attached to the platform base and to the upper
plate by U-joints. Since the rods of pistons are al-
lowed to rotate about their axes, the upper U-joints
perform as ball joints. The pistons are controlled by
motion control modules hosted by an IBM PC. Elec-
trohydraulic servo valves regulate the flow of oil
into the hydraulic pistons, and thus determine the
piston’s extension velocity. An amplifier converts
the voltage signal produced by the motion control
module into the current required to drive the elec-
trohydraulic servo valves. An optical encoder pro-
vides the velocity and position feedback signal of
each leg to the motion control module.19
The geometry of the FAU Stewart platform. a
The geometries of the base and the moving
plate are given in Figure 3, where the base joints are
denoted by B and the platform joints by P . The
nominal parameters of the platform were measured
crudely by tape; these are given in Table I. Further-
more, the nominal joint offsets were set to be the
nominal length of the six legs when the platform
The pose measurement technique employed in this
paper was reported in ref. 20. To measure poses of
the platform, three targets were placed on the plate,
as shown in Figure 3a. These targets defined the

centroid was defined as the origin of

theodolite and then transformed to the plate coordi-
P . Similarly, to determine the trans-
formation from the theodolite coordinate system to

base, as shown in Figure 3b. These three targets
defined the base coordinate system
were on the x axis of B , target G

An important mathematical problem is to find a
transformation that maps a set of a minimum of
three 3D point measurements from one coordinate
system to another. For instance, assume that the
three targets on the plate are measured by the
theodolite. Thus their coordinates are given with

Before the calibration experiments were performed,
both the accuracy of the measurement procedure
and the repeatability of the Stewart platform itself
A target was mounted on a CNC machine tool
and moved to several known locations within the
machine workspace. The position of the target at
each location was measured following the proce-
dure outlined in ref. 20, using the theodolite. Two
standard commercial measuring tapes with a reso-
lution of 1r16 in. were used in this experiment. The
distance between the target locations was calculated
using the obtained measurements and then com-
pared with the reading from the CNC machine
controller. It was observed that the discrepancy be-
tween the CNC readings and the theodolite mea-
surements was about 0.005 in. This point measure-
The coordinates of base targets in B and T .
Journal of Robotic Systems—1998
ment accuracy is adequate for our experimental
studies on Stewart platform calibration.
The repeatability of the platform was deter-
mined by repeatedly moving the platform to a num-
ber of predetermined poses. These poses were mea-
sured with the theodolite. It was found that the
repeatability of the platform was about 0.1 in. which
defined the upper bound of accuracy enhancement
At this point, the platform was moved to 23
different poses within the range of "10 in. in the
x, y, z directions and "15T of the roll, pitch, and
yaw angles. The coordinates of the three targets on
the platform were also measured with the theodo-
lite, and then the corresponding poses were com-
puted with the algorithm summarized in Section
5.2. Among these, it was found that three poses
were outliers and therefore were discarded. We
suspect that these outliers were mainly due to hu-
man factors, as none of the measurement process
was automated. The remaining 20 poses were trans-
formed from the theodolite coordinate system to the
platform base coordinate system.
The minimum number of poses for the estima-
tion of the platform parameters is seven, since there
are 42 parameters. However, as a rule of thumb,
twice as many as the minimum required number of
pose measurements need to be used for kinematic
identification. Thus, among the 20 measured poses,
12 poses were used to identify the kinematic param-
eters of the platform, and the other eight were
saved for verification of the identification results.
The Gauss]Newton algorithm was used to
identify the platform parameters, where the identifi-
cation Jacobian was completed by Eq. 8 . The algo-
rithm converged in a few iterations, as shown in
Figure 4, and the final values of the identified
parameters are given in Table III. Figure 5 shows
Norm of the error parameter vector vs. number
the convergence of the average measurement resid-
uals the difference between the measured and the
computed leg lengths , which indicates that the al-
gorithm converges to the proper solution.
For the purpose of verification, the identified
parameters, together with the measured leg lengths,
were substituted into the platform kinematic model
to produce eight verification poses, which were then
compared with the measured poses. The results of
this comparison are given in Table IV, where d and
d denote position and orientation error vectors of
the platform pose, respectively. Position errors are
in inches, and orientation errors in radians.
The identified parameters of the FAU Stewart platform from the
Levenberg]Marquardt algorithm.
Zhuang, Yan, and Masory: Calibration of Stewart Platforms
Average leg length errors vs. number of itera-
The following comments can be made on the
rapidly, since the error model is well struc-
tured and the identification Jacobian is well
conditioned. It was observed that the condi-
tion number was about 100, which is consid-
ered small in comparison to calibrating a
Puma by an error-model based technique. In
that case we witnessed that the condition
number was at least 10 times larger. This
indicates that the proposed algorithm is ro-
2. The results given in Table IV indicate that
the accuracy of the Stewart platform after
calibration was enhanced to near the level of
its repeatability. This indicates that the cali-
bration method is very effective, and that the
measurements obtained by the theodolite are
sufficiently accurate for this particular pur-
pose. One can further improve the accuracy
of the platform by first enhancing its repeata-
bility and then by a stringent calibration.
A method for the calibration of Stewart platforms
has been derived and demonstrated in this paper.
This method is based on formulating a cost function
with leg length errors, and, as a result, has a few
advantages, such as 1 The resulting error model is
Stewart platform forward kinematic problem, re-
sulting in a very efficient and robust kinematic
identification algorithm. 3 The scaling problem of
balancing the contribution of position errors and
orientation errors in the cost function is eliminated.
If the cost function is formulated in terms of pose
errors of the platform, this scaling problem always
exists. 4 The identification Jacobian is a block diag-
onal matrix; therefore error parameters of each leg
can be solved independently. In this way, the nu-
merical efficiency of the algorithm is improved.
We were attempting to investigate analytically
the observability of error parameters with the help
of the identification Jacobian. The expression be-
came formidable after we attempted to find the
conditions for the dependency of the columns of the
Jacobian matrix. It is still possible, however, to study
numerically the error parameter observability by
using this identification Jacobian.
It has also been shown that the proposed method
can be extended to other parallel manipulators
without much effort. As a principle, one should
always formulate the cost function in terms of joint
errors rather than pose errors whenever the inverse
kinematic solution of a manipulator is simpler than
its forward kinematic solution.
Both the proposed calibration and measurement
methods were tested on a large platform, and it was
shown that the pose inaccuracy of that platform was
reduced to about 0.2 in, which is near its repeatabil-
ity. The poor repeatability in this case is mainly due
to error sources such as clearances between the
Pose errors of the calibrated platform model from the

joints, compliance of the legs, and uncertainties in
the home positioning. Some of the identified error
parameters are extremely large because of the small
orientational displacement and the low repeatability
As a footnote, a unified formulation for the
calibration of both serial- and parallel-link manipu-
lators was first proposed in ref. 21 and was then
extended in ref. 22. In a follow-up work, we at-
tempted to perform self-calibration on the Stewart
platform, and the results were reported in ref. 23
